analysis.stack_summary: Blazor Server + ASP.NET Core Web API (same host) + Dapper + SQL Server + JWT. This is a pragmatic, low-abstraction stack. AI performs best on stacks where cause and effect are traceable without inspecting framework internals.
ai_strengths: Dapper: SQL is explicit and readable. AI can generate, read and modify queries without understanding a query-translation layer.
Blazor Server: component lifecycle is simple and synchronous. AI can reason about state without WASM boundary complexity.
Same-host API+UI: no CORS, no separate auth token propagation between projects. One auth surface.
SQL Server: well-represented in AI training data. Schema-first approach maps cleanly to Dapper.
.NET 9 / C# 13: primary records, pattern matching, and minimal API syntax are compact and AI-generatable.
ai_weaknesses: Dapper has no change tracking: AI cannot infer which fields changed. UPDATE statements must be fully explicit — risk of silent data loss if AI omits a column.
Blazor Server circuit model: AI frequently confuses Blazor Server lifecycle (OnInitializedAsync, StateHasChanged) with Blazor WASM. Risk of subtle state bugs.
JWT + custom auth (no ASP.NET Identity): AI defaults to Identity scaffolding. Every auth-related prompt must explicitly state 'no ASP.NET Identity'.
Multi-tenancy without ORM global filters: AI-generated queries will regularly forget the tenant WHERE clause. This is the single highest risk.
Shared DbConnection lifecycle: AI tends to open connections per-operation or per-request inconsistently without guidance.

risks.description: Dapper has no global query filter. Every AI-generated SELECT must manually include WHERE CustomerId = @customerId. AI will omit this under time pressure or when context window is short.
risks.mitigation: Wrap all Dapper calls in a typed IQuerySession(customerId) that injects the tenant parameter at the call site. Make it impossible to query without a tenant context object.

risks.description: AI generates UPDATE SET col1=@v1, col2=@v2 based on the fields it sees in the current prompt. Fields outside the prompt context get silently dropped.
risks.mitigation: One SQL file per operation (not inline strings). AI edits the file, not an interpolated string. Reviewer sees the full SQL diff.

risks.description: AI will inject HttpContext.User.Claims directly in handlers instead of using a typed ICurrentUser service. This couples handlers to HTTP and breaks unit tests.
risks.mitigation: Define ICurrentUser with UserId, CustomerId, ProfileId, IsImpersonating. Inject it everywhere. Never reference HttpContext outside middleware.

risks.description: AI frequently calls StateHasChanged() from async callbacks on wrong thread, or forgets InvokeAsync wrapper. Causes silent render failures.
risks.mitigation: Establish a BaseComponent with a SafeStateHasChanged() wrapper and document it in the prompt template.

risks.description: AI adds pipeline behaviors (validation, logging, auth) but does not know the correct registration order. Wrong order causes auth bypass or double-logging.
risks.mitigation: Register behaviors in a single PipelineRegistration.cs file with numbered comments. AI is instructed to add to this file only.

risks.description: AI opens IDbConnection per-handler without using-blocks, or registers it as singleton instead of transient/scoped.
risks.mitigation: Provide an IDbSession factory abstraction. Register as scoped. Handlers receive IDbSession, not IDbConnection directly.

architecture_recommendation.pattern: Vertical Slice Architecture with explicit tenant context

[Domain]/.[Feature]HandlerTests.cs: unit test per handler, NSubstitute IDbSession

architecture_recommendation.key_rule: One .sql file per database operation. No inline SQL strings. No dynamic query builders unless isolated in a named QueryBuilder class.

dapper_strategy.query_location: Each feature slice has its own .sql file embedded as an assembly resource. Handler loads it via a SqlLoader helper. AI edits the .sql file, not C# strings.
dapper_strategy.tenant_enforcement: IDbSession wraps IDbConnection and exposes QueryAsync<T>(string sql, object param, TenantContext tenant). TenantContext is non-optional. The wrapper appends AND CustomerId = @_tenantId to every query automatically via a convention (all tables have a CustomerId column).
dapper_strategy.update_safety: UPDATE statements use explicit column lists in the .sql file. AI must modify the .sql file to add/remove columns. The diff is visible in source control.
dapper_strategy.concurrency: Add RowVersion (timestamp) column to all mutable entities. Handler reads current RowVersion, passes it in WHERE clause of UPDATE. If 0 rows affected → return ConcurrencyError from Result<T>.
dapper_strategy.unit_testing: Mock IDbSession with NSubstitute. Return fake data from QueryAsync. No TestContainers required for unit tests. Integration tests use a real DB with Respawn for reset.
dapper_strategy.transactions: IDbSession.BeginTransaction() returns an IDbTransaction. Handlers that touch multiple tables receive IDbSession and call BeginTransaction explicitly. Never assume ambient transaction.

principles: ICurrentUser is the only way to get the current user in any handler or component. Never read HttpContext.User directly.
JWT generation lives in exactly one class: JwtTokenService. No other class creates tokens.
Refresh token rotation: single-use, stored in UserRefreshToken table, invalidated on use. One class: RefreshTokenService.
All auth flows are explicit commands: LoginCommand, RefreshTokenCommand, SamlCallbackCommand, ImpersonateCommand.
SAML2 and MSAL are adapters that terminate in the same internal LoginCommand. The handler does not know which auth method was used.
2FA is a second MediatR step: LoginCommand returns RequiresTwoFactor result (not a token). Client calls VerifyTwoFactorCommand to get the token.
Impersonation sets an additional ImpersonatedUserId claim. ICurrentUser exposes IsImpersonating and OriginalUserId. All audit entries include both.
auth_strategy.blazor_integration: CustomAuthenticationStateProvider reads the JWT from localStorage via JSInterop on first render. It starts a background timer that calls RefreshTokenCommand 2 minutes before expiry. On circuit disconnect the timer is disposed.
auth_strategy.permission_check: await _authz.CanAsync(userId, profileId, Permission.X) — single method, single SQL query, result cached per request in ICurrentUser scope.
auth_strategy.ai_safety: Every auth prompt must begin with: 'Do not use ASP.NET Identity. Do not use IdentityServer. Use ICurrentUser from SharedKernel/Auth/. Tokens are issued by JwtTokenService only.'

tradeoffs.benefit: SQL is visible, diffable, and AI-readable. No LINQ-to-SQL translation surprises.
tradeoffs.downside: No global query filters, no change tracking, no migrations. Schema changes require manual SQL scripts.
tradeoffs.why_preferred: AI generates better explicit SQL than implicit LINQ. Hidden ORM behavior is the leading cause of AI-generated bugs in data access.

tradeoffs.benefit: AI can understand and modify one feature without reading the entire codebase. All files for a feature are in one folder.
tradeoffs.downside: Some logic duplication across slices (e.g. permission checks). Requires discipline to not create horizontal shared services.
tradeoffs.why_preferred: AI context windows are finite. A self-contained slice fits in context. A layered architecture requires reading Controller + Service + Repository + Model simultaneously.

tradeoffs.decision: Co-located Blazor pages with API slices
tradeoffs.benefit: The Blazor page, its command, and its handler are in the same folder. AI sees the full feature in one pass.
tradeoffs.downside: Mixes server-side and client-side concerns in the same project.
tradeoffs.why_preferred: Same-host Blazor Server eliminates the client/server boundary. The co-location benefit outweighs the conceptual mixing.

tradeoffs.benefit: AI cannot pass a CustomerId where a ProfileId is expected. Compiler catches cross-ID bugs immediately.
tradeoffs.downside: Boilerplate per ID type. JSON serialization requires custom converters.
tradeoffs.why_preferred: AI-generated code frequently mixes numeric IDs. Strongly-typed IDs make the error non-compilable rather than a silent runtime bug.

tradeoffs.benefit: Handler return types are explicit contracts. AI can see all possible outcomes without reading exception documentation.
tradeoffs.downside: More verbose than throw. AI trained on exception-based code may revert to throws.
tradeoffs.why_preferred: Every prompt must include: 'Return Result<T>. Never throw for business errors.' Once established, AI follows consistently.

tradeoffs.benefit: SQL is version-controlled, reviewable, and independently editable. AI edits SQL without touching C#.
tradeoffs.downside: More files per feature. SqlLoader helper required.
tradeoffs.why_preferred: Inline SQL strings are invisible to code review tools and AI context scanners. File-per-operation is the single highest-impact AI safety measure for Dapper.

final_recommendation.stack: Keep the baseline stack. No changes required.
additions: Add Scrutor (assembly scanning for handler registration)
Add Respawn (database reset for integration tests)
removals: Do NOT add EF Core — even for migrations. Use Apply-Migrations.ps1 (sqlcmd-based) for SQL-file migrations.
Do NOT add ASP.NET Identity — even as a reference. Its presence confuses AI prompts.
structural_non_negotiables: ICurrentUser — never expose HttpContext.User outside middleware
IDbSession — never expose raw IDbConnection to handlers
One .sql file per database operation — no inline SQL strings
ITenantContext injected into IDbSession — no WHERE CustomerId written in handlers
PipelineRegistration.cs — single ordered list of MediatR behaviors
final_recommendation.prompt_header_for_every_feature: Use vertical slice architecture. Place all files in Features/[Domain]/[Feature]/. Use ICurrentUser from SharedKernel/Auth/. Use IDbSession from SharedKernel/Db/. Load SQL from [Feature].sql via SqlLoader. Return Result<T> — never throw for business errors. Do not use ASP.NET Identity or EntityFramework.

breakdown.clarity: 9 — explicit SQL, explicit auth, explicit tenant context
breakdown.safety_for_ai_edits: 7 — Dapper UPDATE risk mitigated by .sql files; tenant risk mitigated by IDbSession wrapper
breakdown.predictability: 8 — vertical slices mean every feature looks identical
breakdown.maintainability_via_prompts: 8 — self-contained slices fit in AI context window

ai_score.ceiling_reason: Score cannot reach 10 because Blazor Server circuit model and Dapper multi-tenant enforcement require prompt discipline that cannot be fully automated. A score of 8 is realistic and achievable with the mitigations described.
